sub_points: Course overview: lecture format, consultation channels, and instructor introductions/backgrounds
Learning outcomes and expectations for student competencies in pattern recognition
Assessment structure and grading components; recommended references and study materials
Professional conduct: academic integrity/code of conduct and course policies
Updated syllabus highlights and newly introduced course elements

syllabus.topic: Introduction to Pattern Recognition and the Machine Learning Landscape
sub_points: Definition and scope of pattern recognition; typical PR pipeline/system diagram and components
Relationship and distinctions among PR, machine learning, AI, and deep learning
Common ML framework: problem setup, data/labels, model learning vs inference, and output forms
Reasoning modes (deductive vs inductive) and how they map to learning from data
Learning paradigms (e.g., supervised/unsupervised and other common variants) and their goals
Interpretations of probability (frequentist vs Bayesian) and implications for modeling
Core probability axioms and properties for events (union, intersection, complements)
Random variables: discrete vs continuous; PMF/PDF and basic usage in modeling
Linking probabilistic modeling to uncertainty handling in recognition systems

syllabus.topic: Bayes’ Rule and Probabilistic Inference (with Worked Examples)
sub_points: Bayes’ rule formulation: priors, likelihoods, evidence, and posterior probabilities
Interpretation of Bayes’ rule as an inference/update mechanism from data to belief
Applying Bayes’ rule to practical scenarios (e.g., weather forecasting) to compute posteriors
Diagnostic testing example: sensitivity/specificity, base rates, and posterior risk intuition
Understanding how prior probabilities affect decisions under uncertainty

syllabus.topic: Discrete Distributions for Classification: Bernoulli and Binomial
sub_points: Bernoulli distribution for binary outcomes: parameterization and expectation intuition
Binomial distribution as repeated Bernoulli trials: assumptions, PMF, and key properties
Connecting Bernoulli/Binomial models to counting events and label generation
Worked example using the binomial model to compute event probabilities
Recap consolidation of Bayes rule and discrete distribution concepts

syllabus.topic: Predictive Modeling for Discrete Outputs: Sigmoid, Categorical/Multinomial, and Softmax
sub_points: Modeling binary targets using probabilistic prediction; role of the sigmoid/logistic link
Geometric/functional interpretation of the sigmoid and probability outputs in [0,1]
Categorical and multinomial distributions for multi-class outcomes and their parameters
Softmax function for multi-class probability prediction and normalization behavior
Key softmax properties and how they support stable multi-class inference

syllabus.topic: Continuous Distributions and Parametric Modeling: Univariate Gaussian and Others
sub_points: Univariate Gaussian distribution: parameters (mean/variance) and shape/scale intuition
How Gaussian assumptions support modeling continuous-valued measurements/features
Overview of other common univariate distributions and when they may be appropriate
Comparing distributional choices and their impact on likelihood-based modeling

syllabus.topic: Model Fitting via Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
sub_points: Probability model estimation as a fitting problem: choosing parameters to explain data
Maximum likelihood principle: likelihood, log-likelihood, and optimization perspective
Closed-form MLE for Bernoulli (estimating class probability) from sample statistics
Closed-form MLE for univariate Gaussian (estimating mean and variance) from data

syllabus.topic: Learning as Optimization: Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) and Misclassification Risk
sub_points: ERM framework: minimizing average loss on observed data as a proxy for expected risk
Choice of loss functions and how they encode learning objectives (e.g., classification error)
Minimizing misclassification rate and implications for decision boundaries
Positioning ERM as a foundation for later supervised learning algorithms

source_file: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf
